sections.heading: Definition and Anatomic Sites
sections.content: Biliary tract cancers (BTCs) are a heterogeneous group of highly lethal malignancies arising from the epithelial lining of the biliary tree. This encompasses cancers originating from the gallbladder, intrahepatic bile ducts (within the liver parenchyma), and extrahepatic bile ducts (which include the perihilar/hilar and distal bile ducts).

sections.heading: Embryology and Anatomy
sections.content: The biliary system is embryologically derived from the foregut endoderm. Anatomically, the biliary tree is divided into the intrahepatic and extrahepatic components. The gallbladder stores bile, while the intrahepatic bile ducts collect bile from the liver and merge to form the right and left hepatic ducts, which unite outside the liver to form the common hepatic duct. The cystic duct from the gallbladder joins the common hepatic duct to form the common bile duct, which drains into the duodenum.

sections.heading: Histopathologic Classification and Spread
sections.content: BTCs are collectively defined as adenocarcinomas or other histologic types arising from this system. They are characterized by local invasion, vascular invasion, regional lymph node metastasis, and distant metastases.

sections.heading: Anatomic Subtype Frequency
sections.content: Gallbladder cancer is the most common anatomic subtype, followed by extrahepatic CCA, which is more common than intrahepatic CCA [MS-2, MS-9].
